1: <header>Environmental impact of heavy pig production in a sample of Italian farms. A cradle to farm-gate analysis </header>
G. Pirlo a,* S. Carè a , G. Della Casa b , R. Marchetti b, , G. Ponzoni b, V. Faeti b, V. Fantin C , P. Masoni P. Buttol L. Zerbinatti d , F. Falconi e

<list>a Council for Agricultural Research and Economics, Fodder and Dairy Production Research Centre, Via A. Lombardo 11, 26900 Lodi, Italy 
b Council for Agricultural Research and Economics, Research Unit for Swine Husbandry, Via Beccastecca 345, San Cesario sul Panaro, 41018, Italy 
C ENEA, Italian National Agency for New Technologies, Energy and Sustainable Economic Development, Via Martiri di Monte Sole 4, 40129 Bologna, Italy 
d Associazione Regionale Allevatori dell'Emilia Romagna, Via Roma 89/2-Loc. Viadagola, 40057 Granarolo dell'Emilia, Italy 
e LCA-lab SRL, Bologna, Italy</list>
2:
<list>Pig production has relevant effects on water and air quality, global warming and natural resource depletion. 
An LCA was performed on the produc- tion of heavy pig (> 160 kg of body weight) production. 
There is a wide variability of environ- mental performances among farms. 
It is shown that there is wide margin of reduction of environmental impact of heavy pig production.</list>
3: <header>A R T I C L INFO </header>
Article history: Received 19 February 2016 Received in revised form 21 April 2016 Accepted 24 April 2016 Available online 17 May 2016
Keywords: Global warming potential Eutrophication Acidification Photochemical ozone formation Abiotic resource depletion
4: <header>ABSTRACT </header>

Four breeding piggeries and eight growing-fattening piggeries were analyzed to estimate potential environmen- tal impacts of heavy pig production (> >160 kg of live height at slaughtering). Life Cycle Assessment methodology was adopted in the study, considering a system from breeding phase to growing fattening phase. Environmental impacts of breeding phase and growing-fattening phase were accounted separately and then combined to obtain the impacts of heavy pig production. The functional unit was 1 kg of live weight gain. Impact categories investi- gated were global warming (GW), acidification (AC), eutrophication (EU), abiotic depletion (AD), and photo- chemical ozone formation (PO).

The total environmental impact of 1 kg of live weight gain was 3.3 kg CO2eq, 4.9 E kg SO2eq, 3.1 E-2 kg PO eq, 3.7 E-3 - kg Sbeq, 1.7 E-3 kg C2H4eq for GW, AC, EU, AD, and PO respectively. Feed production was the main hotspot in all impact categories. Greenhouse gases responsible for GW were main- ly CH4, N20, and CO2. Ammonia was the most important source of AC, sharing about 90%. Nitrate and NH3 were the main emissions responsible for EU, whereas P and NOx showed minor contributions. Crude oil and natural gas consumption was the main source of AD. A large spectrum of pollutants had a significant impact on PO:

they comprised CH4 from manure fermentation, CO2 caused by fossil fuel combustion in agricultural operations and industrial processes, ethane and propene emitted during oil extraction and refining, and hexane used in soy- bean oil extraction. The farm characteristics that best explained the results were fundamentally connected with performance indicators Farms showed a wide variability of results, meaning that there was wide margin for im- proving the environmental performance of either breeding or growing-fattening farms. The effectiveness of some mitigation measures was evaluated and the results that could be obtained by their introduction have been presented.

7: <header>1. Introduction </header>

According to IPQ-INEQ (2014) approximately 13 million pigs were slaughtered in Italy in 2013 and about 61% of them were used for the production of the Protected Designation Origin (PDO) hams, which are obtained according to the Regulations of Prosciutto di Parma or Pro- sciutto di San Daniele, ruling on every production step. In the last de- cades, pig production has been increasing considerably in the northern regions of Italy, and four regions (Piemonte, Lombardia, Vene- to and Emilia-Romagna) currently account for approximately 83% of total pig population (ERSAF, 2014). Intensification of the production system and concentration of pigs in large farms on restricted areas have increased the environment load and have caused several accept- ability problems, due to emissions contributing to climate change and affecting human and environment health. The underlying feed produc- tion requires also the consumption of non-renewable resources and the combustion of fossil fuels for agricultural operations as well as the syn- thesis and extraction of inorganic fertilizers. The environmental burden of the pork sector in Europe-27 accounts for approximately 25% of total animal greenhouse gases (GHG) emissions, on a life cycle assessment (LCA) basis (Weiss Leip, 2012). Several European directives regu- late intensive pig production. For example, Directive 2010/75/EU of the European Parliament (Directive IED on industrial emissions) specif- ically addresses the installation of intensive rearing poultry and pig plants, with the aim to reduce emissions to air, soil and water and to im- prove resource efficiency, by adopting the best available techniques. Ni- trate Council Directive (91/676/EEC) is another fundamental tool for preventing the excess of nutrients from agriculture (namely N) which pollute surface and groundwater. Intensive pig production is also sub- ject to the National Emission Ceilings Directive (2001/81/EC), aiming to control the airborne emissions of SO2, NOx, VOCs and NH3. Finally, similarly to any economic activity causing GHG emissions, pig produc- tion should contribute to achieve the goals of the Paris Agreement.

The environmental impact of pig production has been evaluated in several literature LCA studies (Table 1). Most of them are restricted to the agricultural phase and do not consider slaughter and processing phases. The survey shows that in all these studies, with the exception
Table 1 Literature LCA studies of pig production.

Authors|Country|Final weight (kg) Basset-Means and van der Werf (2005)|FR|113-150 Bava et al. (in press)|IT|169 Bonesmo et al. (2012)|NO|92 Cederberg and Flysjö (2004)|S|105-160 Cherubini et al. (2015)|BR|125 Dalgaard et al. (2007)|DK|109-140 Dourmad et al. (2014)|| Garcia-Launay et al. (2014).|FR|115 Gonzalez et al. (2012)|E|150 González-García et al. (2015)|PO|105 Halberg et al. (2010)|FR|100 Lammers et al. (2010)|USA|136 Pelletier et al. (2010)|USA|110-126 Reckmann et al. (2013)|D|129 Sagastume Gutièrez et al. (2015)|CU|120 Stone et al. (2012)|USA|118

of Cederberg and Flysjö (2004), the final live weights are much lower than 160 kg, and therefore they are not fully representative of typical Italian heavy pigs. In fact, the abovementioned Regulations of PDO hams set the genotypes suitable for this production and the minimum age (9 months) and live weight (160 kg) at slaughtering Consequently, these pigs must grow slower than the lighter pigs, which represent the most of worldwide production. Moreover, the use of a variety of feed- stuffs (namely fats and oils) and byproducts is severely restricted in the first growing period (up to 80 kg of live weight) and are forbidden in the finishing period. Therefore, it is clear that both nutrient require- ments (in particular energy to protein ratio) and feed formulations of heavy pig are quite different from those of younger and lighter pigs pro- duced all over the world and that this specificity can influence environ- mental load, because feed efficiency and body weight are inversely correlated (Della Casa et al., 2009). The first study on this characteristic production system has been published very recently (Bava et al., in press).

Because of both the scarcity of quantitative data about the environ- mental impact of heavy pig production >160 kg of live weight and 9 months of age) and the need to environmentally assess the mitigating strategies that can be adopted, the Italian Ministry of Agriculture, Food and Forestry funded a specific LCA study with the aim to address global warming (GW), acidification (AC) and eutrophication (EU), the most representative environmental impact categories of this production. This paper presents the results of the LCA study and includes also the as- sessment of the depletion of abiotic resources (AD), because an efficient use of resources is a key point for sustainability, and of the photochem- ical ozone formation (PO), as an indicator of air quality and responsible for significant effects on human health.

12: Life cycle assessment is a standardized methodology (ISO 14040, 2006; ISO 14044, 2006) for analyzing environmental potential impacts of a product or a service throughout life cycle. It applies a "cradle to grave" approach which includes extraction and processing of raw mate- rials, manufacturing, product distribution, use, maintenance, recycling and final disposal. According to the ISO 14040 (2006) and ISO 14044 (2006), an LCA study consists of four main steps: 1) goal and scope def- inition, 2) life cycle inventory analysis, 3) life cycle impact assessment and 4) interpretation of results. In step 1, the purpose of the study, sys- tem boundaries, functional unit and assumptions are defined. Step 2 consists of a detailed compilation of all inputs (material and energy con- sumption) and outputs (products, airborne and waterborne emissions as well as waste generated) from the processes at each stage of the product's life cycle. Step 3 aims to assess potential environmental im- pacts based on the life cycle inventory results. Finally, the results are an- alyzed and interpreted in step 4.
13: <header>2.1. Goal of the study and decision context </header>

The goal of the study was to assess the environmental impacts of the production of heavy pig up to the farm gate and to identify the hotspots in the production chain. Two separated analyses were performed in this study: the first one concerning the breeding phase for the piglet produc- tion and the second one the growing-fattening phase; successively, the

results of the two analyses were combined together to obtain the envi- ronmental impact of whole production chain. An attributional approach was applied, according to the archetype A decision context as defined in the ILCD Handbook (European Commission, 2010).
14: The system investigated was the heavy pig rearing for the produc- tion of Italian cured hams, which comprises two phases: breeding phase for the production of piglet and growing-fattening phase. For each phase, the system boundaries are from cradle to farm gate (Fig. 1) encompassing feed production, both on- and off-farm, and the activ- ities related to pig feeding and breeding phases. More in detail, direct and indirect consumption and emissions were attributed to the follow- ing activities within the boundaries:

<list>enteric fermentation from the fermentative processes of all animal categories; 
manure management, including emissions due to manure storage; 
- manure application, including N and P field emissions from animals'</list>

<list>- feed production, both on- and off-farm feeds, including production of seeds, fertilizers and pesticides, field emissions due to their appli- cation and agricultural operations, consumption of electricity, fuel and other materials for farm activities; 
- transports of piglets, purchased feeds, chemical fertilizers and pesti- cides to the farms; 
recyclable products, which refer to manure exceeding the require- ments of on-farm feed production.</list>

Emissions from land use change were not included in the bound- aries, because there were no changes in land management. Packaging of purchased products, their disposal treatments and incidental use of veterinary products were assumed to have an insignificant contribution to environmental impacts when compared to the entire life cycle (Stone et al., 2012). The assessment of the impact of buildings and maintenance of facilities and infrastructures was out of the scope of this study, whose object was to identify environmental hotspots of the production processes.

16: The functional unit was defined as 1 kg body weight gain for both the two production systems (breeding phase and growth and fattening phase) and the fattened pig system (whole chain).

Culled SOWS are a co-product of piglet production. Economic alloca- tion according to the market price was adopted to estimate the contri- bution of such co-product. This decision has been taken considering that the ratio of prices of sow meat and finished pig meat is substantially steady among years.

Some farms examined in the study produce manure exceeding the requirements of on-farm feed production. In these cases, the surplus of manure was utilized by neighboring vegetable producers as organic fertilizer and it was assumed that exceeding manure were utilized as substitute for urea and the avoided impacts of this production were cal- culated (European Commission, 2010).
17: Four breeding and eight fattening pig farms, located in the province of Modena, were involved in this study. The sample of farms was repre- sentative of breeding and growing pig farms operating in this area, which is one of the most important for this economic sector in Italy. Pri- mary data referring to 2011 and concerning feed production and live- stock management were collected at each farm by means of questionnaires including the following items: number of animals for each category; housing system; manure collection, handling and stor- age system; consumption and type of fertilizers and pesticides; diet at each sub-phase; length of the sub-phases and weight at the beginning and at the end of every sub-phase; amount and type of purchased feeds; electricity and fuels. Secondary data mainly refer to Italian condi- tions and calculation methodologies. The Ecoinvent database (v.2.0; Swiss Centre for Life Cycle Inventories, 2007) and European Reference Life Cycle Database (European Commission, JRC-IES, 2013) were used for background data. Ecoinvent database was used for the production of off-farm feeds. The study was carried out by using SimaPro 7.3.3 soft- ware (Pré Consultants, 2013).
Size and specific characteristics of each breeding farm are shown in Table 2; electricity, methane and diesel consumption of each breeding

22: The following impact categories have been used for evaluating the environmental impacts of pig production:

<list>a) Global warming, which refers to an increase in average global tem- peratures, caused primarily by increases in GHG. 
b) Depletion of abiotic resources, which are non-renewable resources such as minerals and fossil fuels. 
c) Photochemical ozone formation, which refers to the degradation and oxidation under the influence of sunlight of solvents and volatile organic compounds when they are released into the atmosphere. 
d) Acidification, which refers to the decrease of pH in water and soil due to acid depositions. 
e) Eutrophication, which refers to the potential impact of enriching aquatic ecosystems with nutrients, especially N and P compounds. The surplus N and P in aquatic ecosystems leads to both the growth of algae and the decomposition of dead organic material which de- crease oxygen levels in water.</list>

Some of these impact categories, such as global warming, ozone de- pletion and abiotic resource depletion, have global relevance because their environmental effects are not influenced by regional conditions. Some others are more relevant for the area where the sources of emis- sions are located and consequences for the environment are influenced by local condition. This is the case of acidification and eutrophication, which have site-specific effects on air and water quality and are influ- enced by the local concentration of the emissions sources.
Environmental impacts have been calculated according to CML 2 Baseline 2000 method (CML, 2009) and the corresponding characteriza- tion factors of the main elementary flows are reported in Table 5.

27: Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) Tier 1 methodol- ogy was applied to estimate methane emissions from enteric fermenta- tion and from manure management, N excretion and N20 emissions from manure in the piggeries (Cóndor et al., 2008; Cóndor, 2011).

Dinitrogen monoxide airborne emissions due to N fertilizers applica- tion were estimated according to Cóndor et al. (2008). More in detail, their estimation included both direct emissions caused by application of manure and N synthetic fertilizers and indirect emissions, following N leaching and run-off and re-deposition of volatilized gases to soil and waters.
Cóndor (2011) was adopted to estimate yearly NH3 emissions from the pig houses and from manure application and to estimate and nitrate waterborne emissions.
Emission factors used in life cycle inventory analysis are shown in Table 5.
28: <header>3. Results and discussion </header>
3.1. Environmental impacts

Table 6 shows the average results of the LCA study of 1 kg of piglet body weight gain in the four breeding farms (results of each breeding farm are reported in Appendix Table S5. Results of AD and PO showed wide variability, whereas GW, AC and EU have similar outcomes. No

farm could be found showing the best performance in every environ- mental impact category. Culled-sows share to all environmental impact categories coincided with their economic contribution (Table rang- ing from 1.9% of farm 4 to 3.1% of farm 1. These values are considerably lower than those assumed by Basset-Means and van der Werf (2005) which ranged between 6% and 7%, probably because of the lower final weight of fattened pigs or because the ratio between the prices of 1 kg of body weight of fattened pigs and that of SOWS is lower than the ratio recorded in the present study.

The average environmental impacts associated with the production of 1 kg of body weight of fattened pig are also reported in Table 6. This phase comprises only pig growing and fattening and it does not include the environmental burden of piglet. Similarly to breeding phase, there was a wide variability among farms, especially for AD and PO.

The environmental impact of the whole production chain has been calculated for an average fattened pig on the basis of the average pig weight at the end of each phase (breeding and fattening) and then re- ferred to the functional unit (1 kg of body weight). The results are shown in Table 6 (results of each growing-fattening farm are reported in Appendix Table S6). > 70% of the environmental impact can be attrib- uted to the growing and fattening phase (Fig. 2), due to the longer dura- tion of this period if compared to that of the breeding phase and to the high nutrient requirements of the finishing animals. Fernández et al. (1999), who analyzed the production of a standard pig of about 100 kg body weight, estimated that sows, weaners and growing pigs contributed 22%, 13% and 63% of N excretion and 26%, 15% and 59% of P excretion, respectively. These data have also been confirmed by Reckmann et al. (2013) who found a contribution of fattening phase ranging from 62% to 69% for GW, AC and EU. The lower contribution of growing-fattening phase in that study if compared to the present LCA is explained by the lower final weight.

The contribution analysis of the farm activities to environmental im- pact is a key step for finding the hotspots and designing a mitigation strategy. Fig. 3 shows the contribution of the breeding-farm activities to GW, AC, EU, AD, and PO. Transport did not contribute significantly to GW and both enteric fermentation and consumption of fuel, electric- ity and other materials for farm activities showed minor contributions; manure management accounted for 30% to 42% of total GHG emissions; manure application contributed approximately 18% in all farms; howev- er, feed production was also an important source of GHG: 10% to 22% of total GHG came from feeds for gilts and SOWS and 15% to 30% from those for piglets.

The main contributor of AC was manure management, which accounted for about 60% of total AC in all breeding farms. Manure appli- cation was another important source of AC (20% of the total), whereas the other activities showed minor contributions.

In nearly all breeding farms manure management and manure appli- cation contributed about 20% and 18% to EU, respectively. The role of feed production was variable: production of feeds for SOWS and piglets contributed from 10% to 22% and from 5 to 20% of total EU, respectively.

Feed production for SOWS and piglets and consumption of energy, fuel and materials were the main contributors of AD; consumption at breeding farm 2 accounted for nearly 100% of AD, due to its high re- quirements for electricity and fuels for heating and cooling. As regards recyclable products, significant negative values of AD can be observed in all farms, due to the reuse of exceeding manure, which can result in an indirect saving of energy and minerals, for the production of urea.

Manure management and sow feed production were appreciable sources of PO; however, the major contributor to PO was piglet feed production in all farms, with the exception of breeding farm 1, which had a different ratio between the amount of soybean meal fed to SOWS and to piglets if compared to the other breeding farms.

As regards the growing-fattening farms, the contributions of the farm activities to the LCA results of the production of 1 kg of finished pig are reported in Fig. 4. Feed production gave the highest contribution to total GW (62.5% on average) due to manure and fertilizer application

Impact category|Unit|Piglet|Piglet|(growing-fattening) Heavy pig|(growing-fattening) Heavy pig|Heavy pig (whole chain) Impact category|Unit|Mean|CV (%)|Mean|CV (%)|Mean Global warming|kg CO2eq|4.20|8.78|3.15|9.63|3.3 Acidification|kg SO2eq|6.48 E-2|5.29|4.30 E-2|16.2|4.9 E-2 Eutrophication|kg PO-eq|3.74 E-2|8.48|2.86 E-2|11.1|3.1 E-2 Abiotic depletion|kg Sbeq|3.91 E-3|22.9|3.75|29.3|3.7 E-3 Photochemical ozone formation|kg C2H4eq|2.14 E-3|28.5|1.62|28.9|1.7 E

and agricultural operations. The importance of feed production in af- fecting GW was emphasized by Reckmann et al. (2013), who showed that about 65% of GHG are emitted from this activity. Dourmad et al. (2014) showed that the contribution of feed production to GW is affect- ed by production system, being the lowest in a conventional system (67% approximately) and the highest in an organic one (75% approxi- mately). Manure management and manure application also played an important role, with very little variations among farms, and the values we observed are very similar to those reported by Dourmad et al. (2014) when GHG emissions from pig house, manure management and manure application are considered together.

From 50% to 60% of AC was due to manure management; pig feed production and manure application were also appreciable sources of AC, with about 20-30% and 10-30%, respectively. The other farm activ- ities did not give any remarkable contribution to AC.
Feed production was the main contributor to EU, with about 50-65% of the total, whereas manure management and manure application accounted for 20-30% of the total.

Feed production had a remarkable contribution to AD, because, sim- ilarly to GW, many non-renewable resources, such as mineral fertilizers or fossil fuels are used in agricultural operations. The contribution of pig feed production exceeded 100% in most farms, because it did not con- sider the non-renewable resources that were saved as exceeding ma- nure and were utilized outside the growing-fattening farms. The values exceeding 100% were compensated with the negative contribu- tion of recyclable products, varying from - 20% to - 80%, with the ex- ception of growing-fattening farm 6.
Pig feed production had a significant contribution to PO, varying from 60% to 90% of the total.
29: <header>3. Results and discussion </header>
3.1. Environmental impacts

Table 6 shows the average results of the LCA study of 1 kg of piglet body weight gain in the four breeding farms (results of each breeding farm are reported in Appendix Table S5. Results of AD and PO showed wide variability, whereas GW, AC and EU have similar outcomes. No
Table 5
Emission factor used in life cycle assessment inventory.

Emission|Emission factor Enteric CH4|1.5 kg/year pig Manure management|22.6 kg/year boar or sow CH4|7.94 kg/year gilt CH4|8.35 kg/year pig growing-fattening phase N excreted|5.0 kg/year pig between 20 and 50 kg of body weight N excreted|9.9 kg/year pig between 50 and 80 kg of body weight 14.5 kg/year pig between 80 and 110 kg of body weight N excreted|20.6 kg/year pig 110 kg of body weight Manure N20|0.001 kg N-N20/kg of N excreted Piggery NH3|4.87 kg/year sow Piggery NH3|2.41 kg/year other categories Manure application NH3|3.08 kg/year sow Manure application NH3|1.39 kg/year sow NO3|0.3 kg/kg of N excreted

farm could be found showing the best performance in every environ- mental impact category. Culled-sows share to all environmental impact categories coincided with their economic contribution (Table rang- ing from 1.9% of farm 4 to 3.1% of farm 1. These values are considerably lower than those assumed by Basset-Means and van der Werf (2005) which ranged between 6% and 7%, probably because of the lower final weight of fattened pigs or because the ratio between the prices of 1 kg of body weight of fattened pigs and that of SOWS is lower than the ratio recorded in the present study.

The average environmental impacts associated with the production of 1 kg of body weight of fattened pig are also reported in Table 6. This phase comprises only pig growing and fattening and it does not include the environmental burden of piglet. Similarly to breeding phase, there was a wide variability among farms, especially for AD and PO.

The environmental impact of the whole production chain has been calculated for an average fattened pig on the basis of the average pig weight at the end of each phase (breeding and fattening) and then re- ferred to the functional unit (1 kg of body weight). The results are shown in Table 6 (results of each growing-fattening farm are reported in Appendix Table S6). > 70% of the environmental impact can be attrib- uted to the growing and fattening phase (Fig. 2), due to the longer dura- tion of this period if compared to that of the breeding phase and to the high nutrient requirements of the finishing animals. Fernández et al. (1999), who analyzed the production of a standard pig of about 100 kg body weight, estimated that sows, weaners and growing pigs contributed 22%, 13% and 63% of N excretion and 26%, 15% and 59% of P excretion, respectively. These data have also been confirmed by Reckmann et al. (2013) who found a contribution of fattening phase ranging from 62% to 69% for GW, AC and EU. The lower contribution of growing-fattening phase in that study if compared to the present LCA is explained by the lower final weight.

The contribution analysis of the farm activities to environmental im- pact is a key step for finding the hotspots and designing a mitigation strategy. Fig. 3 shows the contribution of the breeding-farm activities to GW, AC, EU, AD, and PO. Transport did not contribute significantly to GW and both enteric fermentation and consumption of fuel, electric- ity and other materials for farm activities showed minor contributions; manure management accounted for 30% to 42% of total GHG emissions; manure application contributed approximately 18% in all farms; howev- er, feed production was also an important source of GHG: 10% to 22% of total GHG came from feeds for gilts and SOWS and 15% to 30% from those for piglets.

The main contributor of AC was manure management, which accounted for about 60% of total AC in all breeding farms. Manure appli- cation was another important source of AC (20% of the total), whereas the other activities showed minor contributions.

In nearly all breeding farms manure management and manure appli- cation contributed about 20% and 18% to EU, respectively. The role of feed production was variable: production of feeds for SOWS and piglets contributed from 10% to 22% and from 5 to 20% of total EU, respectively.

Feed production for SOWS and piglets and consumption of energy, fuel and materials were the main contributors of AD; consumption at breeding farm 2 accounted for nearly 100% of AD, due to its high re- quirements for electricity and fuels for heating and cooling. As regards recyclable products, significant negative values of AD can be observed in all farms, due to the reuse of exceeding manure, which can result in an indirect saving of energy and minerals, for the production of urea.

Manure management and sow feed production were appreciable sources of PO; however, the major contributor to PO was piglet feed production in all farms, with the exception of breeding farm 1, which had a different ratio between the amount of soybean meal fed to SOWS and to piglets if compared to the other breeding farms.

As regards the growing-fattening farms, the contributions of the farm activities to the LCA results of the production of 1 kg of finished pig are reported in Fig. 4. Feed production gave the highest contribution to total GW (62.5% on average) due to manure and fertilizer application

Feed production was the main contributor to EU, with about 50-65% of the total, whereas manure management and manure application accounted for 20-30% of the total.

Feed production had a remarkable contribution to AD, because, sim- ilarly to GW, many non-renewable resources, such as mineral fertilizers or fossil fuels are used in agricultural operations. The contribution of pig feed production exceeded 100% in most farms, because it did not con- sider the non-renewable resources that were saved as exceeding ma- nure and were utilized outside the growing-fattening farms. The values exceeding 100% were compensated with the negative contribu- tion of recyclable products, varying from - 20% to - 80%, with the ex- ception of growing-fattening farm 6.
Pig feed production had a significant contribution to PO, varying from 60% to 90% of the total.
33: <header>3. Results and discussion </header>
3.1. Environmental impacts

Table 6 shows the average results of the LCA study of 1 kg of piglet body weight gain in the four breeding farms (results of each breeding farm are reported in Appendix Table S5. Results of AD and PO showed wide variability, whereas GW, AC and EU have similar outcomes. No
Table 5
Emission factor used in life cycle assessment inventory.

Emission|Emission factor Enteric CH4|1.5 kg/year pig Manure management|22.6 kg/year boar or sow CH4|7.94 kg/year gilt CH4|8.35 kg/year pig growing-fattening phase N excreted|5.0 kg/year pig between 20 and 50 kg of body weight N excreted|9.9 kg/year pig between 50 and 80 kg of body weight 14.5 kg/year pig between 80 and 110 kg of body weight N excreted|20.6 kg/year pig 110 kg of body weight Manure N20|0.001 kg N-N20/kg of N excreted Piggery NH3|4.87 kg/year sow Piggery NH3|2.41 kg/year other categories Manure application NH3|3.08 kg/year sow Manure application NH3|1.39 kg/year sow NO3|0.3 kg/kg of N excreted

farm could be found showing the best performance in every environ- mental impact category. Culled-sows share to all environmental impact categories coincided with their economic contribution (Table rang- ing from 1.9% of farm 4 to 3.1% of farm 1. These values are considerably lower than those assumed by Basset-Means and van der Werf (2005) which ranged between 6% and 7%, probably because of the lower final weight of fattened pigs or because the ratio between the prices of 1 kg of body weight of fattened pigs and that of SOWS is lower than the ratio recorded in the present study.

The average environmental impacts associated with the production of 1 kg of body weight of fattened pig are also reported in Table 6. This phase comprises only pig growing and fattening and it does not include the environmental burden of piglet. Similarly to breeding phase, there was a wide variability among farms, especially for AD and PO.

The environmental impact of the whole production chain has been calculated for an average fattened pig on the basis of the average pig weight at the end of each phase (breeding and fattening) and then re- ferred to the functional unit (1 kg of body weight). The results are shown in Table 6 (results of each growing-fattening farm are reported in Appendix Table S6). > 70% of the environmental impact can be attrib- uted to the growing and fattening phase (Fig. 2), due to the longer dura- tion of this period if compared to that of the breeding phase and to the high nutrient requirements of the finishing animals. Fernández et al. (1999), who analyzed the production of a standard pig of about 100 kg body weight, estimated that sows, weaners and growing pigs contributed 22%, 13% and 63% of N excretion and 26%, 15% and 59% of P excretion, respectively. These data have also been confirmed by Reckmann et al. (2013) who found a contribution of fattening phase ranging from 62% to 69% for GW, AC and EU. The lower contribution of growing-fattening phase in that study if compared to the present LCA is explained by the lower final weight.

The contribution analysis of the farm activities to environmental im- pact is a key step for finding the hotspots and designing a mitigation strategy. Fig. 3 shows the contribution of the breeding-farm activities to GW, AC, EU, AD, and PO. Transport did not contribute significantly to GW and both enteric fermentation and consumption of fuel, electric- ity and other materials for farm activities showed minor contributions; manure management accounted for 30% to 42% of total GHG emissions; manure application contributed approximately 18% in all farms; howev- er, feed production was also an important source of GHG: 10% to 22% of total GHG came from feeds for gilts and SOWS and 15% to 30% from those for piglets.

The main contributor of AC was manure management, which accounted for about 60% of total AC in all breeding farms. Manure appli- cation was another important source of AC (20% of the total), whereas the other activities showed minor contributions.

In nearly all breeding farms manure management and manure appli- cation contributed about 20% and 18% to EU, respectively. The role of feed production was variable: production of feeds for SOWS and piglets contributed from 10% to 22% and from 5 to 20% of total EU, respectively.

Feed production for SOWS and piglets and consumption of energy, fuel and materials were the main contributors of AD; consumption at breeding farm 2 accounted for nearly 100% of AD, due to its high re- quirements for electricity and fuels for heating and cooling. As regards recyclable products, significant negative values of AD can be observed in all farms, due to the reuse of exceeding manure, which can result in an indirect saving of energy and minerals, for the production of urea.

Manure management and sow feed production were appreciable sources of PO; however, the major contributor to PO was piglet feed production in all farms, with the exception of breeding farm 1, which had a different ratio between the amount of soybean meal fed to SOWS and to piglets if compared to the other breeding farms.

As regards the growing-fattening farms, the contributions of the farm activities to the LCA results of the production of 1 kg of finished pig are reported in Fig. 4. Feed production gave the highest contribution to total GW (62.5% on average) due to manure and fertilizer application

Feed production was the main contributor to EU, with about 50-65% of the total, whereas manure management and manure application accounted for 20-30% of the total.

Feed production had a remarkable contribution to AD, because, sim- ilarly to GW, many non-renewable resources, such as mineral fertilizers or fossil fuels are used in agricultural operations. The contribution of pig feed production exceeded 100% in most farms, because it did not con- sider the non-renewable resources that were saved as exceeding ma- nure and were utilized outside the growing-fattening farms. The values exceeding 100% were compensated with the negative contribu- tion of recyclable products, varying from - 20% to - 80%, with the ex- ception of growing-fattening farm 6.
Pig feed production had a significant contribution to PO, varying from 60% to 90% of the total.
37: The results of this study are quite aligned with those of other LCA studies (Reckmann et al., 2012). As regards GW, the relatively low re- sults of this study can be partially explained in several ways. We

Fig. 2. Contributions of breeding and fattening phases to global warming (GW), acidification (AC), eutrophication (EU), depletion of abiotic resource (AD), and photochemical ozone formation (PO) of entire pig production chain.

adopted an economic criterion for allocating the contribution of sow meat production, whereas the other studies did not consider this kind of contribution, with the exception of Basset-Means and van der Werf (2005), who adopted an economic allocation, and Pelletier et al. (2010) and Cherubini et al. (2015), who adopted a physical allocation. We also excluded the post-farm phases, that were instead included by Reckmann et al. (2013), Cherubini et al. (2015) and Dalgaard et al. (2007) and represent 1.0% to 7.0% of the GW associated to entire pig production chain. Although the main responsible for GHG emissions is N20 in monogastrics, another fact that should be considered for explaining the results of our study is that CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation and manure management were estimated by adopting default emission factors for category, irrespective of the final body weight. Nitrogen excretion influences all impact categories and the re- sults are determined by the single emission factor used for pigs weighting 110 kg. Cederberg and Flysjö (2004) obtained very high values of GW, AC and EU for a final live weight of 160 kg. Although they found slightly lower environmental impacts for lighter final live weights, no linear relationship between live weight and environmental impact could be found. Moreover, these authors did not use emission factors depending on the live weight, and their results are likely to be af- fected by the different production systems they examined.

The small size of farm samples and some different methodological choices affect the comparability of the results obtained in our study and in Bava et al. (in press). In our study the use of an economic alloca- tion for culled SOWS reduces the environmental impact results of body weight gain of fattened pig. We observed lower GW and, on the contrary higher values of AC than in Bava et al. (in press), probably due to some differences in housing and manure management systems, different CH4 and N20 emission factors correspond to. In our study, both materials and fossil fuels consumption is included in AD (whereas they are con- sidered in two different impact categories in Bava et al. (in press).

40: Mortality rate was positively correlated with EU (coefficient of de- termination 0.91 and P = 0.05) and PO (coefficient of determination 0.99 and = 0.09). This parameter is a good indicator of the production efficiency which can explain the environmental burden associated with a product unit. Similarly, a negative relationship was found between piglet's average daily gain and AC (coefficient of determination 0.82 and P = 0.09), because the piglet contribution to N compounds was lower if they reach the final weight rapidly. The positive correlation be- tween AD and piglet weight (coefficient of determination 0.83 and P = 0.09) was due to the same reasons. All the characteristics having a sig- nificant relationship with the LCA results of the breeding phase are indi- cators of the production efficiency obtained either by improving hygienic, nutritional and well-being condition of the piggery or by re- ducing the length of the breeding phase.

As regards growing-fattening farms, AC and EU results can be ex- plained by the growing-fattening length (coefficient of determination 0.67 and P = 0.01 and coefficient of determination 0.54 and = 0.04 re- spectively); these impact categories are mainly affected by animal N emission factors, so that the longer the animal is kept in the piggery

Fig. 3. Contribution of enteric fermentation (ENF), manure management (MM), manure application (MA), SOW feed production (SF), piglet feed production (PF), consumption of electricity, fuel and other materials for animal care (CON), input transport to farm (INT), and recyclable products (RP) to global warming (GW), acidification (AC), eutrophication (EU), depletion of natural resource (AD), and photochemical ozone formation (PO) in the breeding farms.

the higher the N airborne and waterborne emissions. The farm charac- teristic that most influenced PO was feed efficiency (coefficient of deter- mination 0.78 and P = 0.003), which considered different aspects of efficiency, as mortality rate, growing-fattening length and average daily gain. On the other hand, no particular farm characteristic was found to give a sound explanation of GW and AD results. We expected a positive effect of growing rate on GW and AD, but differences among fattening farms were too small and they were counterbalanced by other characteristics, such as consumption of fossil fuels for feed production.
3.4. Main sources of environmental impacts and mitigation strategies

Table 7 shows the main emissions from both phases affecting GW, AC, EU, AD, and PO. Only sources contributing at least 2% to impact cat- egories are reported. As regards GW, these results were very similar to those obtained by Reckmann et al. (2013). Methane was mostly emitted from manure inside the pig house or during storage. N20 originated from the N compounds excreted by animals or from the nitrogen fertil- izers used for feed production. Philippe and Nicks (2015) recently reviewed the extensive literature concerning biological and technical

factors affecting emissions of GHG in pig production. They mentioned mitigation measures concerning manure management, such as type of floor, manure removal strategy, manure stocking systems, use of straw, and feeding management, such as reduction of protein consump- tion, dietary fiber, and use of feed additives. According to these authors, the most effective measure is to increase the frequency of manure re- moval, whereas the reduction in protein intake is effective for decreas- ing N20 emissions but does not affect CH4 emissions (Hansen et al., 2014). In the present study, the main source of CO2 emissions was com- bustion of fossil fuels used in agricultural operations, crop drying and synthesis of nitrogen fertilizers, which were included in feed production activities in Figs. 3 and 4. Reckmann et al. (2013) estimated that 81% of CO2 emissions are due to feed production. Therefore, the main strategy for reducing CO2 emissions is to improve feed efficiency, but minimum tillage practice that reduces agricultural operations (Kim et al., 2009) or efficient use of synthetic fertilizers (Ma et al., 2012) are also of major importance.

Ammonia was by far the most important source of AC (Table 7 Sev- eral mitigation measures can be implemented, which are partially com- mon to those that can be adopted for N20 (Philippe et al., 2011). In particular, some studies demonstrated that N excretion can be reduced

fattening piggeries had a three-phase feeding systems and no breeding or growing-fattening farm used synthetic amino acids.

The main sources of EU were NO3 and NH3, whereas P had a minor contribution (Table 7), consistently to what was observed by Cederberg and Flysjö (2004). Nitrous oxide, NH3, and NO3 are affected by both N excretion from animal and chemical and biochemical factors. Similarly to GW and AC, improvement of feed protein efficiency is the main strat- egy for reducing NO3 and NH3. However, the reduction of NO3 leaching could be achieved by timing manure or fertilizer application with vege- table growth.

In both phases of pig production, the main source of AD was crude oil, because diesel is used in agricultural operations and for electricity production. However, the consumption of natural gas at farms had also a remarkable contribution. A reduction of AD can be obtained by decreasing diesel consumption at farm or improving the efficiency in the use of feeds. Strategies for reducing energy consumption can be dif- ferentiated between breeding and growing-fattening farms. We have seen (Fig. 3) that about 60% of AD in breeding farms was due to con- sumption of fuel, electricity and other material for animal care, whereas, (Fig. 4), nearly all AD of growing-fattening farms was due to feed production. In both farm types, the use of manure as fertilizer, in substitution of synthetic fertilizers (recyclable products in Figs. 3 and 4), allowed savings of considerable amount of abiotic resources. Anaer- obic digestion of manure (in combination or not with other types of bio- mass) is a very effective strategy for producing renewable energy and reducing abiotic resources use (Nguyen et al., 2010). Since a wide vari- ability of natural gas consumption was observed across breeding farms (Table 3), the efficiency of energy use can be improved, for example by natural ventilation, optimization of mechanical ventilation systems, use of low energy lighting and energy-saving systems for piglet heating (European Commission, 2003).

Photochemical ozone formation was affected by different processes (Table 7). In addition to CH4 emitted from manure and enteric fermen- tation, the elementary flows mainly affecting PO were ethane and

3.5. How much best practices improve environmental performances of breeding and growing-fattening piggeries

The study has shown that there are wide margins of improvement of environmental performances of both breeding and growing-fattening piggeries and there are some strategies that potentially mitigate envi- ronmental impacts of pig meat production. Since AC and EU are the en- vironmental categories with the highest attention of farmers and policymakers nowadays, we selected two farms (one breeding and one fattening) that obtained the lowest results for these impact catego- ries and we simulated the introduction of specific measures to improve their environmental profile.

Mortality rate has been found to be a key factor influencing the en- vironmental performances of breeding farms. Among these, BF2 showed the lowest values of AC and EU (Table S5). The simulation aimed to estimate how much is the environmental progress if the mor- tality rate of this farm is as low as that of BF1 (11.8% VS 18.2%, Table 2). This result can be achieved if farmer adopts some best practices, such as a longer suckling period or a reduction of dietary crude protein after weaning, that are considered practices allowing a better health status of piglet gastrointestinal tract. A reduction of mortality rate has a favor- able effect on farm output, with limited impact on inputs; for this rea- son, all the environmental impacts have been reduced by 8% (Table 8).

For growing-fattening phase, optimization of feed strategy has been considered for increasing feed to gain ratio (i.e. kg of feed per kg of live weight gain), which is a key indicator of feed efficiency and affects emis- sions from feed-production activity (Fig. 4), accounting for 30% and 60% of AC and EU respectively. The growing-fattening GFF6 has been select- ed for simulation, and feed to gain ratio was reduced from 3.47 to 3.20 kg of feed per kg of body weight gain, which is considered a very good value of feed efficiency in growing-fattening phase (CRPA, 2015). In order to achieve this result, farmers could adopt a series of best prac- tices, such as changing the feeding system from a single phase to a mul- tiphase system with at least 3 phases, with progressive reduction of crude protein content. This allows to meet better the nutrient require- ments of pigs, to reduce N excretion and NH3 emissions and to optimize digestible energy utilization. The results of the simulation have been re- ported in Table 9; which shows that the extent of the effect varies among impact categories. Reduction of feed to gain ratio has direct
effect on feed and energy consumption, which lowers significantly AD and PO results and, at a less extent, AC and EU because.

||Breeding phase|Breeding phase|phase Growing-fattening|phase Growing-fattening Impact category|Contributor|Mean|CV|Mean|CV Global warming||(%)|(%)|(%)|(%) Global warming|CH4|38.6|14.8|25.1|16.4 Global warming|N20|33.9|7.12|42.9|10.5 Global warming|CO2|24.5|23.5|18.3|13.7 Acidification|NH3|93.5|0.35|89.8|2.10 Acidification|SO2|3.44|11.6|4.52|24.6 Acidification|NOx|2.68|20.0|5.16|17.9 Eutrophication|NO3|58.6|4.31|63.1|2.68 Eutrophication|NH3|35.6|5.17|29.5|6.26 Eutrophication|P|2.79|30.9|5.11|29.13 Eutrophication|NO|1.19|69.9|2.00|17.2 Abiotic depletion|Crude oil|50.5|29.0|67.9|11.2 Abiotic depletion|Natural gas|36.9|42.6|19.2|37.5 Abiotic depletion|Coal|9.45|10.7|11.3|30.3 "Photochemical ozone formation"|CH4|21.3|61.7|12.6|40.3 "Photochemical ozone formation"||||| "Photochemical ozone formation"|CO|21.0|18.7|23.1|11.7 "Photochemical ozone formation"|C2H4 (ethane)|14.0|42.9|14.2|11.9 "Photochemical ozone formation"|C3H6|11.5|42.9|11.0|11.5 "Photochemical ozone formation"|(propene) Hexane|8.00|52.1|12.7|46.0
fattening piggeries had a three-phase feeding systems and no breeding or growing-fattening farm used synthetic amino acids.

The main sources of EU were NO3 and NH3, whereas P had a minor contribution (Table 7), consistently to what was observed by Cederberg and Flysjö (2004). Nitrous oxide, NH3, and NO3 are affected by both N excretion from animal and chemical and biochemical factors. Similarly to GW and AC, improvement of feed protein efficiency is the main strat- egy for reducing NO3 and NH3. However, the reduction of NO3 leaching could be achieved by timing manure or fertilizer application with vege- table growth.

In both phases of pig production, the main source of AD was crude oil, because diesel is used in agricultural operations and for electricity production. However, the consumption of natural gas at farms had also a remarkable contribution. A reduction of AD can be obtained by decreasing diesel consumption at farm or improving the efficiency in the use of feeds. Strategies for reducing energy consumption can be dif- ferentiated between breeding and growing-fattening farms. We have seen (Fig. 3) that about 60% of AD in breeding farms was due to con- sumption of fuel, electricity and other material for animal care, whereas, (Fig. 4), nearly all AD of growing-fattening farms was due to feed production. In both farm types, the use of manure as fertilizer, in substitution of synthetic fertilizers (recyclable products in Figs. 3 and 4), allowed savings of considerable amount of abiotic resources. Anaer- obic digestion of manure (in combination or not with other types of bio- mass) is a very effective strategy for producing renewable energy and reducing abiotic resources use (Nguyen et al., 2010). Since a wide vari- ability of natural gas consumption was observed across breeding farms (Table 3), the efficiency of energy use can be improved, for example by natural ventilation, optimization of mechanical ventilation systems, use of low energy lighting and energy-saving systems for piglet heating (European Commission, 2003).

Photochemical ozone formation was affected by different processes (Table 7). In addition to CH4 emitted from manure and enteric fermen- tation, the elementary flows mainly affecting PO were ethane and

Impact category|Unit|Value from LCA|Value after reduction of mortality rate|Difference (%) Global warming|kg CO2eq/kg live weight gain|4.25|3.91|-8.0 Acidification|kg SO2eq/kg live weight gain|6.18 E-2|5.69|-7.9 Eutrophication|kg PO eq/kg live weight gain|3.40|3.13 E-2|-7.9 Abiotic resource depletion|kg Sbeq/kg live weight gain|4.21 E|3.88 E-3|-7.9 Photochemical ozone depletion|kg C2H4eq/kg live weight gain|2.69|2.48 E-3|-7.9
propene, which are associated with fuel oil extraction and refining. Moreover, LCA results highlighted the contribution of hexane, which is the main solvent used in soybean oil extraction.
3.5. How much best practices improve environmental performances of breeding and growing-fattening piggeries

The study has shown that there are wide margins of improvement of environmental performances of both breeding and growing-fattening piggeries and there are some strategies that potentially mitigate envi- ronmental impacts of pig meat production. Since AC and EU are the en- vironmental categories with the highest attention of farmers and policymakers nowadays, we selected two farms (one breeding and one fattening) that obtained the lowest results for these impact catego- ries and we simulated the introduction of specific measures to improve their environmental profile.

Mortality rate has been found to be a key factor influencing the en- vironmental performances of breeding farms. Among these, BF2 showed the lowest values of AC and EU (Table S5). The simulation aimed to estimate how much is the environmental progress if the mor- tality rate of this farm is as low as that of BF1 (11.8% VS 18.2%, Table 2). This result can be achieved if farmer adopts some best practices, such as a longer suckling period or a reduction of dietary crude protein after weaning, that are considered practices allowing a better health status of piglet gastrointestinal tract. A reduction of mortality rate has a favor- able effect on farm output, with limited impact on inputs; for this rea- son, all the environmental impacts have been reduced by 8% (Table 8).

For growing-fattening phase, optimization of feed strategy has been considered for increasing feed to gain ratio (i.e. kg of feed per kg of live weight gain), which is a key indicator of feed efficiency and affects emis- sions from feed-production activity (Fig. 4), accounting for 30% and 60% of AC and EU respectively. The growing-fattening GFF6 has been select- ed for simulation, and feed to gain ratio was reduced from 3.47 to 3.20 kg of feed per kg of body weight gain, which is considered a very good value of feed efficiency in growing-fattening phase (CRPA, 2015). In order to achieve this result, farmers could adopt a series of best prac- tices, such as changing the feeding system from a single phase to a mul- tiphase system with at least 3 phases, with progressive reduction of crude protein content. This allows to meet better the nutrient require- ments of pigs, to reduce N excretion and NH3 emissions and to optimize digestible energy utilization. The results of the simulation have been re- ported in Table 9; which shows that the extent of the effect varies among impact categories. Reduction of feed to gain ratio has direct
effect on feed and energy consumption, which lowers significantly AD and PO results and, at a less extent, AC and EU because.

Strategies for reducing energy consumption can be dif- ferentiated between breeding and growing-fattening farms. We have seen (Fig. 3) that about 60% of AD in breeding farms was due to con- sumption of fuel, electricity and other material for animal care, whereas, (Fig. 4), nearly all AD of growing-fattening farms was due to feed production. In both farm types, the use of manure as fertilizer, in substitution of synthetic fertilizers (recyclable products in Figs. 3 and 4), allowed savings of considerable amount of abiotic resources. Anaer- obic digestion of manure (in combination or not with other types of bio- mass) is a very effective strategy for producing renewable energy and reducing abiotic resources use (Nguyen et al., 2010). Since a wide vari- ability of natural gas consumption was observed across breeding farms (Table 3), the efficiency of energy use can be improved, for example by natural ventilation, optimization of mechanical ventilation systems, use of low energy lighting and energy-saving systems for piglet heating (European Commission, 2003).

Photochemical ozone formation was affected by different processes (Table 7). In addition to CH4 emitted from manure and enteric fermen- tation, the elementary flows mainly affecting PO were ethane and

3.5. How much best practices improve environmental performances of breeding and growing-fattening piggeries

The study has shown that there are wide margins of improvement of environmental performances of both breeding and growing-fattening piggeries and there are some strategies that potentially mitigate envi- ronmental impacts of pig meat production. Since AC and EU are the en- vironmental categories with the highest attention of farmers and policymakers nowadays, we selected two farms (one breeding and one fattening) that obtained the lowest results for these impact catego- ries and we simulated the introduction of specific measures to improve their environmental profile.

Mortality rate has been found to be a key factor influencing the en- vironmental performances of breeding farms. Among these, BF2 showed the lowest values of AC and EU (Table S5). The simulation aimed to estimate how much is the environmental progress if the mor- tality rate of this farm is as low as that of BF1 (11.8% VS 18.2%, Table 2). This result can be achieved if farmer adopts some best practices, such as a longer suckling period or a reduction of dietary crude protein after weaning, that are considered practices allowing a better health status of piglet gastrointestinal tract. A reduction of mortality rate has a favor- able effect on farm output, with limited impact on inputs; for this rea- son, all the environmental impacts have been reduced by 8% (Table 8).

For growing-fattening phase, optimization of feed strategy has been considered for increasing feed to gain ratio (i.e. kg of feed per kg of live weight gain), which is a key indicator of feed efficiency and affects emis- sions from feed-production activity (Fig. 4), accounting for 30% and 60% of AC and EU respectively. The growing-fattening GFF6 has been select- ed for simulation, and feed to gain ratio was reduced from 3.47 to 3.20 kg of feed per kg of body weight gain, which is considered a very good value of feed efficiency in growing-fattening phase (CRPA, 2015). In order to achieve this result, farmers could adopt a series of best prac- tices, such as changing the feeding system from a single phase to a mul- tiphase system with at least 3 phases, with progressive reduction of crude protein content. This allows to meet better the nutrient require- ments of pigs, to reduce N excretion and NH3 emissions and to optimize digestible energy utilization. The results of the simulation have been re- ported in Table 9; which shows that the extent of the effect varies among impact categories. Reduction of feed to gain ratio has direct
effect on feed and energy consumption, which lowers significantly AD and PO results and, at a less extent, AC and EU because.
11: This paper assessed the environmental life cycle performance of heavy pig production, typical of Italian systems. The growing-fattening phase contributes > 70% in all impact categories. The results are aligned with those of previous studies which considered pigs with lighter live weight. The results can be partially explained by the use of single emis- sion factors for: 1) CH4 from enteric fermentation and manure manage- ment, irrespective of the live weight, and 2) N excretion rate applied to pigs weighing > 110 kg.

Feed production, which comprises several direct and indirect emissions or consumption associated with 1 kg of feed entering the system boundaries, was the farm activity with the highest contribution to all environmental impact categories, at both phases of the production chain.

The farm characteristics that best explained the results were funda- mentally connected with performance indicators, such as mortality rate, average daily gain, or rearing length. Farms showed a wide variability of results, especially for AD and PO. This suggests that the environmental performance of either breeding or growing-fattening farms can be im- proved, in particular by introducing best practices that improve hygien- ic condition in or diet changes and increasing their energy efficiency, fulfilling the requirements of PDO productions at the same time.

Simulations have shown that further improvements can be obtained also in farms with good environmental performances. For example, in the sample of breeding farms examined, the productive performances can be improved by reducing mortality rate. As regards growing-fatten- ing phase an increase of feed efficiency can improve the environmental results per kilogram of live body gain from 2 to 7%, depending on the impact category.

The relevant role of feed production in both phases and for all impact categories implies that feed production systems should be carefully con- sidered for reducing the environmental impact of heavy pig production. This is particularly true for those impact categories that consider water and air quality, because with a more efficient use of feed a less amount of N compounds, either as fertilizers or organic fertilizers, enters the production system, with reduced emissions of N20, NH3 and NO

Finally, it is worth noting that the different methodological choices adopted and the small size of the farm samples in the published LCA studies does not allow a proper discussion about the different results obtained on similar products.
